content.full_text:
Re
Fern PolrrrcAI- PnacrrcEs C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 c 428 J Street . Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
March 12,2002
Frederick G. Soley
City of Vallejo
City Attorney's Offrce
Post Office Box 3068
Vallejo, CA94590-5934
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-01-269
Dear Mr. Soley
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because your question is general in nature and does
not seek advice on a particular proceeding, we provide you with informal assistance.2
QUESTION
Do you, as city attorney, have a conflict of interest disqualifying you from
rendering legal advice to the city's Mobile Home Rent Review Board andlor to the city
council as it reviews the Board's decisions or recommendations, merely because you rent
a mobile home space subject to the Board's jurisdiction?
CONCLUSION
No. Your status as lessee of a mobile home space subject to the Board's juris-
diction does not, by itself; mean that you have a conflict of interest in every decision or
recommendation made by the Board. The fact that you rent a mobile home space does
not disqualify you from rendering advice relative to any matter or proceeding in which
you do not have a "financial interest" within the meaning of the Act.
I Govemrnent Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commissionregulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-
18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistalce does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by a Commission opinion
or forrnal written advice. (See enclosed, Regrrlation 1 83 29(c).)
ei.ee"-


File No. I-01-269
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are the city attorney for the City of Vallejo. You own a mobile home located
in the Carquinez Highlands Mobile Park, one of several mobile home parks in the City of
Vallejo. You rent the space on which the mobile home is located from the owners of the
mobile home park on a month-to-month lease.
The City of Vallejo has a "Mobile Home Rent Review Ordinance" codified as
Chapter 5.64 of the Vallejo Municipal Code. Under the ordinance, tenants of mobile
home parks facing a rent increase may petition for a review of the increase by the Mobile
Home Rent Review Board (the "Board.") Afler holding a public hearing, the Board may
order a park owner to: (a) reduce the rent to arate determined by the Board; (b) continue
the rent as it existed under the former lease or rental agreement; or (c) increase the renl to
a rate set by the Board or to the rate requested by the mobile home park owner. The
parties to a rent review proceeding have the right to appeal the Board's decision to the
Vallejo City Council. The city council may sustain, modify or reverse the Board's
decision. If a mobile home park owner fails to comply with the Board's or council's
order, the city attorney is authorized to seek judicial enforcement.
Chapter 5.64.190 also provides for an "annual review" by the city council of the
Mobile Home Rent Review Ordinance. On these occasions, either the Board or the city
manager may recommend to the city council that the ordinance be amended, and the city
attorney may be asked for legal advice regarding such recommendations.
You are concerned that your status as a mobile home park tenant may create a
conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act should you be called upon to give
advice to city staff the Board, or to the city council in connection with petitions for
review of mobile home park rent increases, or advice relating to recommended
amendments of the ordinance.
ANALYSIS
Your question concerns the Act's conflict of interest provisions, which begin at
$ 87100 by stating the fundamental rule:
"No public official at any level of state or local government
shall make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to
use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest."
Section 87103 indicates that a public official has a financial interest in a decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of his or her economic
interests. As the city attorney, you are a public official subject to the Act's conflict of


File No. I-01-269
Page No. 3
interest provisions. ($ 82048, Regulation 18701 .) Your request presupposes that you
would at least be participating in governmental decisions (see Regulation 18702.1 -
18702.4) when providing legal advice to the Board or city council regarding Board
decisions or recommendations, or contemplating judicial enforcement of Board or city
council decisions. Your question reduces down to this; whether it is foreseeable that any
such decisions would have a material financial effect on any of your economic interests.
To answer this question we must first identify the economic interests potentially
affected by the decisions you describe. There are six kinds of econornic interests from
which conflicts may arise under the Act. They are defined in $ 87103 and Regulations
18703-18703.5 to include any real property in which a public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. ($ 87103(b); Regulation 18703 2.) A public
official also has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or
liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. (The "personal financial
effects" rule, $ 87103 Regulation 18703.5.)
The only potential economic interests we can identify from your request for
advice are the interest in your real property, and your general economic interest in your
personal finances. We treat each in turn.
A. Your economic interest in your real property.
An economic interest in real property includes a leasehold interest. ($ 82033.)
However, Regulation 18233 expressly provides that the statute's terms "interest in real
property" and "leasehold interest" do not include the interest of a tenant in a month-to-
month lease. For purposes of the Act's conflict of interest rules, therefore, your month-
to-month lease in the CarquinezHrghlands Mobile Park is not itself "an interest in real
property."
B. Your economic interest in your personalfinances.
A public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal finances
({ 87103), which are defined to include the official's expenses, income, liabilities, or
assets, as well as those of his or her immediate family. (Regulation i8703.5.) Thus, a
public official may not make, participate in making, or use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect on his or her personal finances. (S 87103 ) A "personal
financial effect" is "material" if it amounts to $250 or more in any 12 monthperiod.
(Regulation 18705.5.)
Although your mobile home park lease is not arealproperty interest within the
meaning of the Act's conflict of interest provisions, you could have a conflict of interest
in any governmental decision that would foreseeably affect the annual rental value of this
lease by $250 or more, which would constitute a material "personal financial effect."


File No. l-01-269
Page No. 4
Your account of the facts does not suggest that typical decisions coming before
the Board or city council would have such an effect but, lacking fulI information on this
point, we simply alert you to the possibility that you would have a conflict of interest in
any decision with a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on your personal
finances or on any other of your economic interests.3
The same considerations are germane to any decisions you might make in
advising the city council on recommended changes to the Mobile Home Rent Review
Ordinance. If under all the pertinent facts and circumstances, it is reasonably foreseeable
that a decision on such a recommendation would have a material financial effect on your
personal finances, or on any of your other economic interests, you have a "financial
interest" in the decision and may not participate therein.a But the fact that you rent a
mobile home space does not, by itself, disqualify you from participating in any decision
in which you do not have a determinable financial interest.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsell*rilJl&
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
LrW.jg
I:\Adviceltrs\01-269
3 An effect of a decision is "reasonably foreseeable," signaling the possibility of a con{lict of interest, r,vhen
tlre effect is "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A
materizrl financial effect need not be a certainty to be "reasonably foreseeable;" a "substantial likelihood"
that it tvill occur is sufficient to meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is onty a mere possibility,
it is not "reasonably foreseeable." (1bld.)
o Unless, of course, the "legally required participation" or "public generally'' exceptions apply to a given
decision. ($$ 87101, 87103, Regulations 18707-18'707.9; i8708.) Your account ofthe facts does not
suggest that these exceptions are applicable to the decisions you anticipate, and we haye not therefore
included them in our analysis.

CONCLUSION: No. Your status as lessee of a mobile home space subject to the Board's juris-
